Child Marriage & Early Education: A Global Crisis

Author

Thomas Bailet

Published

April 15, 2025

Code 
import polars as pl
import geopandas as gp
from plotnine import *

# Chargement des fichiers
indicator1 = pl.read_csv("/content/unicef_indicator_1.csv")
indicator2 = pl.read_csv("/content/unicef_indicator_2.csv")
metadata = pl.read_csv("/content/unicef_metadata.csv")

# Aperçu
print(indicator1.shape)
print(indicator2.shape)
print(metadata.shape)
(785, 14)
(355, 14)
(13715, 15)

Child Marriage & Early Education: A Global Crisis

Introduction

Every year, millions of girls are forced into marriage before the age of 18. This practice, known as child marriage, deprives them of their childhood, their education, and their future. According to UNICEF data, this issue remains deeply rooted in many regions of the world, particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.

At the same time, access to early childhood education — a key driver of long-term development — remains extremely limited in the very same countries. This report explores the link between child marriage and early education deprivation, using UNICEF data across countries and over time.

“Child marriage is a violation of human rights. It robs girls of their childhood and threatens their health, safety and future.” — UNICEF

Key Questions

  • Which countries have the highest rates of child marriage?
  • How has this indicator evolved over time?
  • Is there a relationship between child marriage and access to early education?

1. A World Map of Child Marriage

The map below shows the percentage of women aged 20-24 who were married before age 18, by country. The darker the colour, the more severe the situation. Countries in grey have no available data.

Code 
# Garder uniquement la valeur la plus récente par pays
indicator1_latest = (indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by("country", "alpha_3_code")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("child_marriage_rate"))
)

# Charger le fond de carte
shape_world = gp.read_file(
    "https://public.opendatasoft.com/api/explore/v2.1/catalog/datasets/world-administrative-boundaries/exports/shp"
)

# Jointure avec le shapefile
map_data = shape_world.merge(
    indicator1_latest.to_pandas(),
    left_on="iso3",
    right_on="alpha_3_code",
    how="left"
)

# Carte
(ggplot(data=map_data)
    + aes(fill="child_marriage_rate")
    + geom_map()
    + scale_fill_gradient(low="#ffe5b4", high="#b22222", na_value="lightgrey")
    + coord_fixed()
    + labs(
        title="Child Marriage Rate Around the World",
        subtitle="Percentage of women aged 20-24 married before age 18",
        fill="Rate (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(12, 6))
)

2. The Most Affected Countries

While child marriage is a global issue, some countries face dramatically higher rates than others. The bar chart below highlights the top 15 countries where this practice is most prevalent, revealing that Niger, Central African Republic and Chad are among the most severely affected nations.

Code 
# Top 15 pays avec le taux de mariage précoce le plus élevé
top15 = (indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("child_marriage_rate"))
    .sort("child_marriage_rate", descending=True)
    .head(15)
)

(top15
    >> ggplot(aes(x="reorder(country, child_marriage_rate)", y="child_marriage_rate", fill="child_marriage_rate"))
    + geom_col()
    + coord_flip()
    + scale_fill_gradient(low="#ffe5b4", high="#b22222")
    + labs(
        title="Top 15 Countries with Highest Child Marriage Rates",
        x="Country",
        y="Average Rate (%)",
        fill="Rate (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(10, 7))
)

3. Child Marriage vs. Early Education Access

Is there a link between child marriage and access to early childhood education? The scatterplot below compares both indicators across countries. Each dot represents a country, and the regression line confirms a clear negative relationship: the higher the child marriage rate, the lower the access to early education. This pattern suggests that these two crises reinforce each other, trapping communities in a cycle of poverty and inequality.

Code 
# Préparer les deux indicateurs par pays
marriage = (indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by("country", "alpha_3_code")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("child_marriage_rate"))
)

education = (indicator2
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by("country", "alpha_3_code")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("early_education_rate"))
)

# Jointure des deux indicateurs
scatter_data = (marriage
    .join(education, on="alpha_3_code", how="inner")
)

(scatter_data
    >> ggplot(aes(x="child_marriage_rate", y="early_education_rate"))
    + geom_point(aes(color="child_marriage_rate"), size=3, alpha=0.7)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#b22222")
    + scale_color_gradient(low="#ffe5b4", high="#b22222")
    + labs(
        title="Child Marriage vs. Access to Early Childhood Education",
        x="Child Marriage Rate (%)",
        y="Early Education Access (%)",
        color="Marriage\nRate (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(10, 7))
)

4. Evolution Over Time

Has the situation improved over the years? The time-series chart below tracks the evolution of child marriage rates across different regions of the world. While some regions show a slow but encouraging decline, progress remains far too slow to meet global targets. In Sub-Saharan Africa, rates remain stubbornly high, underlining the urgent need for stronger policies and international support.

Code 
# Joindre indicator1 avec metadata pour avoir le continent
time_data = (indicator1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .join(
        metadata.select(["alpha_3_code", "year"])
        .unique(),
        left_on=["alpha_3_code", "time_period"],
        right_on=["alpha_3_code", "year"],
        how="left"
    )
    .group_by("time_period")
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("avg_marriage_rate"))
    .sort("time_period")
)

(time_data
    >> ggplot(aes(x="time_period", y="avg_marriage_rate"))
    + geom_line(color="#b22222", size=1.2)
    + geom_point(color="#b22222", size=3)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#333333", linetype="dashed")
    + labs(
        title="Global Evolution of Child Marriage Rates Over Time",
        x="Year",
        y="Average Child Marriage Rate (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF"
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(10, 6))
)

Conclusion

This report has highlighted a deeply concerning but improving global situation. Child marriage, while declining overall, remains a major crisis in parts of Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia.

Key findings from this analysis:

  • Niger has the world’s highest child marriage rate, at nearly 80%
  • Countries with high child marriage rates consistently show lower access to early childhood education
  • Globally, the average rate has fallen from ~33% in the 1980s to ~15% today, showing that progress is possible with the right policies

However, the data makes clear that much more needs to be done. Every girl forced into early marriage is a girl denied an education, a future, and a choice. UNICEF and its partners must continue to prioritise girls’ rights, education access, and community engagement to end this practice once and for all.

Data source: UNICEF — unicef_indicator_1.csv, unicef_indicator_2.csv, unicef_metadata.csv
